"Well," he said, "that was surely an extraordinary drink to be given to a person in his condition."

"Not so extraordinary," I replied, "if you bear in mind that in former days, as is commonly said, before the time of Herodicus, the guild of Asclepius did not practise our present system of medicine, which may be said to educate diseases. But Herodicus, being a trainer, and himself of a sickly constitution, by a combination of training and doctoring found out a way of torturing first and chiefly himself, and secondly the rest of the world."

"How was that?" he said.

"By the invention of lingering death; for he had a mortal disease which he perpetually tended, and as recovery was out of the question, he passed his entire life as a valetudinarian; he could do nothing but attend upon himself, and he was in constant torment whenever he departed in anything from his usual regimen, and so dying hard, by the help of science he struggled on to old age."
refashioned:
"Well," he said, "that's surely an extraordinary drink for someone in that condition."

"Not so extraordinary," I replied, "when you consider that before Herodicus, the medical tradition of Asclepius didn't practice our modern system of medicine -- which basically educates diseases into existence. Herodicus was a trainer with a sickly constitution who combined training with doctoring and discovered a way of torturing first himself, then the rest of the world."

"How so?" he said.

"He invented the lingering death. He had a terminal condition that he constantly managed. Since recovery was impossible, he spent his entire life as an invalid, doing nothing but tend to his own health. He was in constant torment whenever he deviated even slightly from his regimen. Through the help of medical science, he struggled painfully all the way to old age."
elegant: Before Herodicus, medicine was simple. Herodicus -- a sickly trainer -- invented the art of managing terminal illness through perpetual medical attention, creating a life of pure invalidism. He didn't cure himself; he merely prolonged his dying, torturing himself and establishing a model that tortured the rest of the world.
summary: Socrates argues that before Herodicus, Asclepian medicine was simple and effective. Herodicus, a trainer with a chronically weak constitution, invented the modern system of perpetual medical management, spending his entire life as an invalid, unable to do anything but tend to his own condition -- a model of lingering death rather than cure.
distilled: Herodicus invented the lingering death: a life spent entirely managing illness rather than living, which corrupted medicine from cure to chronic maintenance.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Herodicus of Selymbria was a historical figure, a gymnastics trainer who combined athletic training with medical regimen -- 'Educate diseases' is a brilliant Platonic coinage: the medical system creates the conditions for the diseases it then treats -- The 'valetudinarian' (someone excessively preoccupied with health) is treated as a cautionary example -- Plato's critique anticipates modern debates about medicalization -- Cross-reference: Protagoras (316d-e) mentions Herodicus; Phaedrus (227d) references his walking regimen -- The Greek medical tradition is here contrasted with itself: early Asclepian medicine (simple, curative) vs. Herodicean medicine (complex, maintenance-based)

narrative:
"A rare reward of his skill!"

"Yes," I said; "a reward which a man might fairly expect who never understood that, if Asclepius did not instruct his descendants in valetudinarian arts, the omission arose, not from ignorance or inexperience of such a branch of medicine, but because he knew that in all well-ordered states every individual has an occupation to which he must attend, and has therefore no leisure to spend in continually being ill. This we remark in the case of the artisan, but, ludicrously enough, do not apply the same rule to people of the richer sort."
refashioned:
"What a fine reward for his expertise!"

"Indeed -- the kind of reward you'd expect for someone who never understood why Asclepius didn't teach his descendants the art of managing chronic illness. The omission wasn't from ignorance or lack of medical skill. It was because Asclepius understood that in a well-ordered society, every person has a job to do and therefore no time to spend being perpetually ill.

We readily accept this principle when it comes to laborers and craftsmen, but absurdly, we don't apply the same standard to the wealthy."
elegant: Asclepius didn't teach chronic illness management not from ignorance but from wisdom: in a well-ordered society, everyone has work to do and no time for perpetual sickness. We accept this for laborers but absurdly exempt the rich.
summary: Socrates explains that Asclepius deliberately omitted teaching chronic illness management because he understood that in a well-ordered state, everyone has work to do and cannot afford to be perpetually ill. This principle is accepted for workers but absurdly not applied to the wealthy.
distilled: Asclepius wisely omitted chronic illness management because a well-ordered society has no room for perpetual invalids, whether rich or poor.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The class distinction is important: the rich are judged more leniently for health-obsession than workers, which Socrates finds 'ludicrous' -- Asclepius is here presented as a statesman-physician who considers social utility -- 'In all well-ordered states every individual has an occupation' restates the specialization principle -- The implicit argument: chronic self-care is a form of idleness that harms the state -- Cross-reference: Book II (369d-370c) on the division of labor; the artisan analogy continues in 3.56

narrative:
"How do you mean?" he said.

"I mean this: When a carpenter is ill he asks the physician for a rough and ready cure; an emetic or a purge or a cautery or the knife, -- these are his remedies. And if some one prescribes for him a course of dietetics, and tells him that he must swathe and swaddle his head, and all that sort of thing, he replies at once that he has no time to be ill, and that he sees no good in a life which is spent in nursing his disease to the neglect of his customary employment; and therefore bidding good-bye to this sort of physician, he resumes his ordinary habits, and either gets well and lives and does his business, or, if his constitution falls, he dies and has no more trouble."
refashioned:
"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean this: when a carpenter gets sick, he asks the doctor for a quick, decisive cure -- an emetic, a purge, cauterization, or surgery. Those are his remedies. If someone prescribes a prolonged dietary regimen and tells him to wrap and coddle his head and follow an elaborate treatment plan, he says at once that he has no time to be sick. He sees no point in a life spent nursing his illness while neglecting his work.

So he says goodbye to that kind of doctor, goes back to his normal habits, and either recovers and gets on with his life, or -- if his body gives out -- he dies and is done with it."
elegant: The carpenter's approach to medicine is admirable in its simplicity: give me a quick cure or let me be. He has no time for prolonged treatment and no interest in a life spent managing illness. He either recovers through normal living or dies without prolonging the inevitable.
summary: Socrates praises the carpenter's pragmatic approach to medicine: he demands a quick, decisive cure and refuses prolonged treatment because he has work to do. If a quick cure doesn't work, he returns to his normal life and either recovers naturally or dies -- either way, he doesn't waste his life nursing his disease.
distilled: The carpenter models the right attitude toward illness: demand a quick cure, refuse prolonged treatment, and get back to work -- or accept death without prolonging the inevitable.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The carpenter is the model patient: pragmatic, no-nonsense, focused on productive work -- The four remedies (emetic, purge, cautery, knife) represent aggressive, decisive intervention -- 'Swathe and swaddle his head' satirizes the elaborate regimens of Herodicean medicine -- The binary outcome (get well or die) is presented as preferable to indefinite invalidism -- This passage has been influential in medical ethics discussions about quality of life vs. length of life -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (478a-479c) on the analogy between medicine for the body and justice for the soul

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and a man in his condition of life ought to use the art of medicine thus far only."

"Has he not," I said, "an occupation; and what profit would there be in his life if he were deprived of his occupation?"

"Quite true," he said.

"But with the rich man this is otherwise; of him we do not say that he has any specially appointed work which he must perform, if he would live."

"He is generally supposed to have nothing to do."

"Then you never heard of the saying of Phocylides, that as soon as a man has a livelihood he should practise virtue?"

"Nay," he said, "I think that he had better begin somewhat sooner."

"Let us not have a dispute with him about this," I said; "but rather ask ourselves: Is the practice of virtue obligatory on the rich man, or can he live without it? And if obligatory on him, then let us raise a further question, whether this dieting of disorders which is an impediment to the application of the mind in carpentering and the mechanical arts, does not equally stand in the way of the sentiment of Phocylides?"

"Of that," he replied, "there can be no doubt; such excessive care of the body, when carried beyond the rules of gymnastic, is most inimical to the practice of virtue."
refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "a man in the carpenter's position should use medicine only to that extent."

"Because he has a job to do," I said. "What good is his life if he can't work?"

"Quite true," he said.

"But the rich man's case is different. We don't say he has any particular work he must perform in order to justify his existence."

"He's generally assumed to have nothing to do."

"Haven't you heard Phocylides' saying: 'Once a man has a livelihood, he should practice virtue'?"

"Actually," he said, "I think he should start somewhat sooner than that."

"Let's not argue with Phocylides about the timing. Instead, let's ask ourselves: is the practice of virtue obligatory for the rich? And if it is, then doesn't this obsessive health management that prevents carpenters from working equally prevent the rich from practicing virtue -- the very thing Phocylides says is their duty?"

"Without a doubt," he replied. "Excessive preoccupation with the body, beyond what good gymnastic requires, is absolutely hostile to the practice of virtue."
elegant: The carpenter can't afford prolonged illness because he has work to do. The rich man supposedly has no work -- but Phocylides says his work is to practice virtue. And excessive health management impedes virtue just as much as it impedes carpentry. Therefore the rich have even less excuse for chronic invalidism.
summary: Socrates extends the argument to the rich: if the carpenter can't afford to be perpetually ill because he has work, the rich person's work is to practice virtue (as Phocylides says). Excessive health management impedes the practice of virtue just as it impedes carpentry, so the wealthy have no excuse for chronic invalidism either.
distilled: The rich man's occupation is virtue; excessive preoccupation with bodily health is as hostile to virtue as it is to carpentry.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Phocylides of Miletus was a 6th-century BC elegiac poet known for gnomic maxims -- His saying implies virtue is a luxury that follows material security -- Glaucon's response ('somewhat sooner') suggests virtue should precede wealth, not follow it -- The key move: even the rich have an 'occupation' (practicing virtue), so they too cannot afford perpetual illness -- Cross-reference: Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics I.8 (1098b-1099a) on external goods needed for virtue; Protagoras (326a-b) on wealth and virtue

narrative: "Yes, indeed," I replied, "and equally incompatible with the management of a house, an army, or an office of state; and, what is most important of all, irreconcilable with any kind of study or thought or self-reflection -- there is a constant suspicion that headache and giddiness are to be ascribed to philosophy, and hence all practising or making trial of virtue in the higher sense is absolutely stopped; for a man is always fancying that he is being made ill, and is in constant anxiety about the state of his body."
refashioned:
"Exactly," I replied. "And it's equally incompatible with managing a household, commanding an army, or holding public office. Most importantly, it's irreconcilable with any kind of serious study, thought, or self-reflection.

The chronic invalid constantly suspects that every headache or dizzy spell is caused by his philosophical exertions. This shuts down any attempt to practice or test virtue at the highest level, because the person is perpetually convinced he's making himself sick, and lives in constant anxiety about his body."
elegant: Chronic health anxiety is incompatible with every serious endeavor -- household management, military command, statecraft. Worst of all, it kills philosophy, because the hypochondriac attributes every headache to thinking and abandons intellectual effort entirely.
summary: Socrates argues that excessive health preoccupation is incompatible with every serious human endeavor -- managing a household, commanding an army, governing, and especially philosophy. The chronic invalid blames every headache on intellectual effort, making serious thought and self-reflection impossible.
distilled: Excessive bodily preoccupation kills philosophy because the hypochondriac blames every discomfort on thinking and abandons intellectual effort.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The progression (household > army > state > philosophy) moves from practical to theoretical, culminating in self-reflection -- 'Headache and giddiness ascribed to philosophy' is both humorous and psychologically acute: psychosomatic symptoms become excuses to avoid intellectual effort -- The passage critiques what moderns might call 'wellness culture' when it displaces productive activity -- Cross-reference: Phaedo (66b-67b) on the body as an obstacle to philosophy; Theaetetus (173c-175b) on the philosopher's indifference to bodily concerns

narrative:
"Yes, likely enough."

"And therefore our politic Asclepius may be supposed to have exhibited the power of his art only to persons who, being generally of healthy constitution and habits of life, had a definite ailment; such as these he cured by purges and operations, and bade them live as usual, herein consulting the interests of the State; but bodies which disease had penetrated through and through he would not have attempted to cure by gradual processes of evacuation and infusion: he did not want to lengthen out good-for-nothing lives, or to have weak fathers begetting weaker sons; -- if a man was not able to live in the ordinary way he had no business to cure him; for such a cure would have been of no use either to himself, or to the State."

"Then," he said, "you regard Asclepius as a statesman."

"Clearly; and his character is further illustrated by his sons. Note that they were heroes in the days of old and practised the medicines of which I am speaking at the siege of Troy: You will remember how, when Pandarus wounded Menelaus, they --"
refashioned:
"Yes, quite likely."

"So our politically minded Asclepius would have used his art only on people who were generally healthy in constitution and habits but had a specific ailment. He would cure them with decisive treatments and tell them to resume their normal lives -- always keeping the state's interests in mind.

But bodies thoroughly penetrated by disease he would not try to cure through prolonged courses of treatment. He had no desire to extend worthless lives or produce weak fathers begetting even weaker sons. If a person couldn't live in the ordinary way, Asclepius saw no point in curing him -- such a cure would benefit neither the patient nor the state."

"So you regard Asclepius as a statesman," he said.

"Clearly. And his sons confirm this. They were heroes of the old days who practiced the kind of medicine I'm describing at the siege of Troy. Remember when Pandarus wounded Menelaus -- they --"
elegant: Asclepius the statesman-physician treated only those who were fundamentally healthy but had specific ailments, curing them decisively and sending them back to work. He refused to prolong the lives of the chronically ill, seeing no benefit to patient or state in extending worthless lives or producing ever-weaker offspring.
summary: Socrates presents Asclepius as a statesman-physician who treated only fundamentally healthy people with specific ailments, using decisive cures. He refused to prolong the lives of the chronically ill, considering such treatment useless to both the individual and the state, and undesirable for producing weak offspring.
distilled: Asclepius as statesman-physician treated only the fundamentally healthy, refusing to prolong worthless lives that served neither the individual nor the state.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage contains Plato's most controversial medical-political argument: that the state has an interest in not prolonging chronically ill lives -- 'Weak fathers begetting weaker sons' introduces a eugenic dimension -- Pandarus wounding Menelaus: Iliad 4.127-49 -- Machaon treats Menelaus: Iliad 4.190-219 -- Asclepius as 'statesman' (politikon) means his medicine serves political/social ends -- Cross-reference: Book V (459d-461c) on selective breeding in the guardian class; Laws 720a-720e on different types of physicians -- This passage has been cited in bioethics debates and is historically significant in discussions of medical rationing

narrative: "Sucked the blood out of the wound, and sprinkled soothing remedies, but they never prescribed what the patient was afterwards to eat or drink in the case of Menelaus, any more than in the case of Eurypylus; the remedies, as they conceived, were enough to heal any man who before he was wounded was healthy and regular in habits; and even though he did happen to drink a posset of Pramnian wine, he might get well all the same. But they would have nothing to do with unhealthy and intemperate subjects, whose lives were of no use either to themselves or others; the art of medicine was not designed for their good, and though they were as rich as Midas, the sons of Asclepius would have declined to attend them."
refashioned:
"...sucked the blood from the wound and applied soothing remedies. But they never prescribed a special diet for Menelaus afterward, any more than for Eurypylus. They believed their treatments were enough to heal anyone who was already healthy and disciplined before being wounded -- even if he happened to drink an inflammatory wine posset, he'd recover just fine.

But chronically unhealthy and intemperate people? They wanted nothing to do with them. Such people's lives were of no use to themselves or to anyone else. Medicine wasn't designed for them, and even if they were as rich as Midas, the sons of Asclepius would have refused to treat them."
elegant: The sons of Asclepius treated wounds with simple remedies and no special diets, trusting that healthy people heal naturally. They refused to treat the chronically unhealthy and intemperate, regardless of wealth -- medicine was not designed for those whose lives serve neither themselves nor others.
summary: Socrates concludes his account of Asclepian medicine: the sons of Asclepius used simple wound treatment without dietary prescriptions, trusting healthy constitutions to heal. They categorically refused to treat chronically unhealthy or intemperate people, regardless of their wealth, because medicine was not designed for those whose lives serve no purpose.
distilled: True medicine treats the fundamentally healthy; it refuses the chronically intemperate regardless of wealth, for their lives serve no purpose to themselves or society.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Sucked the blood and sprinkled remedies' describes Machaon's treatment of Menelaus, Iliad 4.218-19 -- The Eurypylus reference connects back to 3.53 -- 'As rich as Midas' is proverbial: King Midas of Phrygia was legendary for his wealth -- The refusal to treat the rich and intemperate is the most provocative claim: wealth cannot purchase medical treatment in Plato's ideal -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-466a) on the distinction between genuine medicine and flattery; Book III (407d-408b) for the full context of this medical philosophy

narrative:
"They were very acute persons, those sons of Asclepius."

"Naturally so," I replied. "Nevertheless, the tragedians and Pindar disobeying our behests, although they acknowledge that Asclepius was the son of Apollo, say also that he was bribed into healing a rich man who was at the point of death, and for this reason he was struck by lightning. But we, in accordance with the principle already affirmed by us, will not believe them when they tell us both; -- if he was the son of a god, we maintain that he was not avaricious; or, if he was avaricious, he was not the son of a god."
refashioned:
"Those sons of Asclepius were very shrewd."

"Naturally," I replied. "But the tragedians and Pindar defy our principles. Even though they acknowledge Asclepius was the son of Apollo, they also claim he was bribed into healing a rich man who was dying, and that Zeus struck him with lightning for it.

But according to our established principle, we won't accept both claims at once. If he was the son of a god, then he wasn't greedy. If he was greedy, then he wasn't the son of a god. You can't have it both ways."
elegant: The tragedians and Pindar claim Asclepius was both Apollo's son and a corrupt physician bribed into healing the dying rich. By our principle, these claims are incompatible: a god's son cannot be avaricious, and an avaricious man cannot be a god's son.
summary: Socrates applies the earlier principle (divine parentage and vice are incompatible) to the myth of Asclepius: the tragedians and Pindar cannot simultaneously claim he was Apollo's son and that he was bribed into healing a rich man -- these claims are logically incompatible.
distilled: If Asclepius was Apollo's son, he was not greedy; if he was greedy, he was not Apollo's son -- poets cannot maintain both claims.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Pindar tells the story of Asclepius's death in Pythian 3.54-58 -- The tragedians who told this story may include Euripides -- Asclepius was struck by Zeus's thunderbolt for raising the dead (specifically Hippolytus, according to some versions) -- The logical disjunction echoes 3.15 (Theseus and Peirithous) -- Apollo then killed the Cyclopes in retaliation for Zeus killing his son -- Cross-reference: Book II (379b-c) on the principle that evil cannot come from the gods; Pindar Pythian 3 for the full myth

narrative:
"All that, Socrates, is excellent; but I should like to put a question to you: Ought there not to be good physicians in a State, and are not the best those who have treated the greatest number of constitutions good and bad? and are not the best judges in like manner those who are acquainted with all sorts of moral natures?"

"Yes," I said, "I too would have good judges and good physicians. But do you know whom I think good?"

"Will you tell me?"

"I will, if I can. Let me however note that in the same question you join two things which are not the same."
refashioned:
"All that is excellent, Socrates. But I have a question: shouldn't a state have good physicians? And aren't the best physicians those who've treated the widest range of constitutions, healthy and unhealthy? And similarly, aren't the best judges those who've dealt with every kind of moral character?"

"Yes," I said, "I want good judges and good physicians too. But do you know who I consider truly good?"

"Tell me."

"I will, if I can. But first notice that your question conflates two things that aren't the same."
elegant: Glaucon asks whether the best physicians are those with the widest experience of illness, and the best judges those who know every kind of moral character. Socrates agrees in principle but warns that the analogy between physicians and judges is misleading -- the two cases differ fundamentally.
summary: Glaucon asks whether the best physicians and judges are those with the widest experience of their respective domains. Socrates agrees that both are needed but warns that Glaucon's question conflates two fundamentally different cases -- the requirements for good physicians and good judges are not parallel.
distilled: The best physicians and the best judges are both needed, but the qualifications for each differ fundamentally despite apparent parallels.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's question is intelligent: it challenges the idea that physicians and judges should be limited in their experience -- Socrates' response ('two things which are not the same') sets up the distinction developed in 3.63-3.65 -- The physician-judge analogy is important throughout Greek philosophy -- Cross-reference: Gorgias (464b-465d) on the parallel between body and soul arts; Protagoras (313d-314b) on physicians of the soul

narrative:
"How so?" he asked.

"Why," I said, "you join physicians and judges. Now the most skilful physicians are those who, from their youth upwards, have combined with the knowledge of their art the greatest experience of disease; they had better not be robust in health, and should have had all manner of diseases in their own persons. For the body, as I conceive, is not the instrument with which they cure the body; in that case we could not allow them ever to be or to have been sickly; but they cure the body with the mind, and the mind which has become and is sick can cure nothing."
refashioned:
"How so?" he asked.

"Because the cases of physicians and judges are fundamentally different. The most skilled physicians are those who've combined knowledge of their art with the widest experience of disease from youth onward. It's actually better if they aren't robustly healthy themselves -- they should have experienced all kinds of illness in their own bodies.

This makes sense because physicians don't cure the body with their body. If they did, we could never allow them to be or to have been sick. They cure the body with their mind. And a mind that has become sick is incapable of curing anything."
elegant: The best physicians have personal experience of disease because they cure the body with the mind, not with their own body. A physician's bodily sickness enriches his understanding; but a sick mind cannot cure anything.
summary: Socrates explains that physicians benefit from personal experience of disease because they cure the body with the mind, not with their own body. Having been sick themselves enriches their understanding. But this principle does not extend to judges, because a corrupted mind cannot serve justice.
distilled: Physicians cure with the mind, not the body, so personal experience of illness helps them; but a morally corrupted mind cannot serve justice.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The principle 'they cure the body with the mind' is crucial: it justifies physicians having personal experience of illness while setting up the contrast with judges -- A sick body can still house a healthy mind, but a morally sick mind cannot judge rightly -- This passage distinguishes between bodily experience (useful for physicians) and moral experience (harmful for judges) -- Cross-reference: Charmides (156d-157a) on treating the soul to cure the body; Phaedo (64c-67b) on the soul's independence from the body

narrative:
"That is very true," he said.

"But with the judge it is otherwise; since he governs mind by mind; he ought not therefore to have been trained among vicious minds, and to have associated with them from youth upwards, and to have gone through the whole calendar of crime, only in order that he may quickly infer the crimes of others as he might their bodily diseases from his own self-consciousness; the honourable mind which is to form a healthy judgment should have had no experience or contamination of evil habits when young. And this is the reason why in youth good men often appear to be simple, and are easily practised upon by the dishonest, because they have no examples of what evil is in their own souls."

"Yes," he said, "they are far too apt to be deceived."

"Therefore," I said, "the judge should not be young; he should have learned to know evil, not from his own soul, but from late and long observation of the nature of evil in others: knowledge should be his guide, not personal experience."
refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"But with judges it's different. A judge governs mind by mind. Therefore he should not have been raised among corrupt people, associating with criminals from youth, going through the entire catalog of crimes just so he can quickly diagnose the wrongdoing of others the way a physician diagnoses illness from personal experience.

The honorable mind that is to form sound judgments should have had no experience or contamination of evil habits in youth. This is why good young people often seem naive and are easily deceived by the dishonest -- they have no model of evil within their own souls."

"Yes," he said, "they are far too easily deceived."

"Therefore the judge should not be young. He should learn to recognize evil not from his own soul but from long observation of evil in others over time. Knowledge should be his guide, not personal experience."
elegant: A judge governs mind by mind and must therefore not have been contaminated by personal experience of evil. Young good people seem naive because they have no inner model of wickedness. The judge should be old enough to have learned evil through long observation of others, guided by knowledge rather than personal experience.
summary: Socrates explains why judges, unlike physicians, must not learn through personal experience of evil. Since judges govern mind by mind, a corrupted soul cannot judge rightly. Good young people appear naive because they lack inner models of evil. Therefore judges should be mature, having learned about evil through long observation rather than personal moral contamination.
distilled: Judges must learn evil through long observation of others, not personal experience, because a mind corrupted by vice cannot govern other minds justly.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The physician/judge asymmetry is now clear: physicians need personal experience of bodily illness; judges need observational knowledge of moral evil without personal corruption -- 'Good men appear simple' (euetheis) in youth because they lack inner patterns of vice -- This connects to the theory of moral perception: we recognize in others what we know in ourselves -- The principle that judges should be mature anticipates modern judicial appointment practices -- Cross-reference: Meno (77b-78b) on whether anyone desires evil knowingly; Protagoras (352a-358d) on knowledge vs. experience of vice

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the ideal of a judge."

"Yes," I replied, "and he will be a good man (which is my answer to your question); for he is good who has a good soul. But the cunning and suspicious nature of which we spoke, -- he who has committed many crimes, and fancies himself to be a master in wickedness, when he is amongst his fellows, is wonderful in the precautions which he takes, because he judges of them by himself: but when he gets into the company of men of virtue, who have the experience of age, he appears to be a fool again, owing to his unseasonable suspicions; he cannot recognise an honest man, because he has no pattern of honesty in himself; at the same time, as the bad are more numerous than the good, and he meets with them oftener, he thinks himself, and is by others thought to be, rather wise than foolish."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's the ideal judge," he said.

"And he will be a good man -- which answers your original question. A person is good who has a good soul. Now consider the opposite type: the cunning, suspicious person who has committed many crimes and thinks himself a master of wickedness. Among his fellow criminals, he seems brilliant because he judges them by his own nature and takes every precaution.

But put him among virtuous, experienced people, and he looks like a fool. His suspicions are misplaced. He can't recognize an honest person because he has no pattern of honesty within himself. Yet because bad people outnumber good ones and he encounters them more often, he thinks himself wise -- and others think so too."
elegant: The good judge has a good soul. The criminal who fancies himself a master of wickedness is shrewd among criminals but foolish among the virtuous -- he cannot recognize honesty, having no inner pattern for it. Since the bad outnumber the good, he mistakes his cunning for wisdom.
summary: Socrates completes the physician-judge comparison: the good judge is simply a good person with a good soul. The criminal who considers himself a master of wickedness is only clever among fellow criminals; among the virtuous he appears foolish, unable to recognize honesty because he has no internal model for it.
distilled: The criminal is clever among criminals but foolish among the virtuous, because we can only recognize in others what we carry within ourselves.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'He judges of them by himself' is the key psychological principle: we use ourselves as the template for understanding others -- The criminal's 'unseasonable suspicions' among the virtuous = projecting his own dishonesty onto honest people -- 'The bad are more numerous than the good' is a pessimistic empirical claim -- The inability to recognize virtue without possessing it connects to the Platonic principle that like knows like -- Cross-reference: Book VI (490a-b) on the philosopher's love of truth; Theaetetus (176a-b) on evil being more common than good in human affairs

narrative:
"Most true," he said.

"Then the good and wise judge whom we are seeking is not this man, but the other; for vice cannot know virtue too, but a virtuous nature, educated by time, will acquire a knowledge both of virtue and vice: the virtuous, and not the vicious, man has wisdom -- in my opinion."

"And in mine also."

"This is the sort of medicine, and this is the sort of law, which you sanction in your State. They will minister to better natures, giving health both of soul and of body; but those who are diseased in their bodies they will leave to die, and the corrupt and incurable souls they will put an end to themselves."

"That is clearly the best thing both for the patients and for the State."

"And thus our youth, having been educated only in that simple music which, as we said, inspires temperance, will be reluctant to go to law."

"Clearly."

"And the musician, who, keeping to the same track, is content to practise the simple gymnastic, will have nothing to do with medicine unless in some extreme case."

"That I quite believe."

"The very exercises and toils which he undergoes are intended to stimulate the spirited element of his nature, and not to increase his strength; he will not, like common athletes, use exercise and regimen to develop his muscles."

"Very right," he said.

"Neither are the two arts of music and gymnastic really designed, as is often supposed, the one for the training of the soul, the other for the training of the body."

"What then is the real object of them?"

"I believe," I said, "that the teachers of both have in view chiefly the improvement of the soul."

"How can that be?" he asked.

"Did you never observe," I said, "the effect on the mind itself of exclusive devotion to gymnastic, or the opposite effect of an exclusive devotion to music?"

"In what way shown?" he said.

"The one producing a temper of hardness and ferocity, the other of softness and effeminacy," I replied.

"Yes," he said, "I am quite aware that the mere athlete becomes too much of a savage, and that the mere musician is melted and softened beyond what is good for him."
refashioned:
"Most true," he said.

"So the good and wise judge we're seeking is not the criminal, but the virtuous man. Vice cannot truly know virtue, but a virtuous nature, educated by time and experience, acquires knowledge of both virtue and vice. Wisdom belongs to the virtuous, not the vicious -- in my opinion."

"And in mine."

"This, then, is the kind of medicine and the kind of law our state sanctions. They will serve better natures, providing health of both soul and body. Those whose bodies are hopelessly diseased will be left to die, and the corrupt and incurable souls will be put to death."

"That is clearly best for both the patients and the state."

"And our youth, educated in the simple music that inspires temperance, will rarely feel the need for courts."

"Clearly."

"And the person who follows the same path with simple gymnastic will rarely need medicine, except in emergencies."

"I quite believe it."

"His exercises and exertions are designed to stimulate the spirited element of his nature, not merely to build muscle. He won't train like an ordinary athlete, using diet and exercise just to develop his body."

"Very right," he said.

"In fact, music and gymnastic are not really designed -- as people commonly think -- one for the soul and the other for the body."

"Then what are they really for?"

"I believe both arts aim primarily at improving the soul."

"How can that be?" he asked.

"Haven't you noticed the effect on the mind of exclusive devotion to gymnastic? Or the opposite effect of exclusive devotion to music?"

"In what way?"

"One produces hardness and ferocity; the other, softness and weakness."

"Yes, I'm well aware that the pure athlete becomes too savage, and the pure musician becomes soft beyond what's healthy."
elegant: Vice cannot know virtue, but virtue, educated by time, knows both -- so wisdom belongs to the virtuous. In our state, medicine and law serve better natures; the hopelessly diseased and incurably corrupt are let go. Both music and gymnastic aim at the soul, not (as commonly supposed) one at the soul and the other at the body. Exclusive gymnastic produces savagery; exclusive music produces weakness. The balance of both is needed.
summary: Socrates establishes that wisdom belongs to the virtuous (who can understand both virtue and vice), outlines the state's harsh policy toward the incurably sick and corrupt, and reveals a surprising thesis: both music and gymnastic are designed for the soul, not (as commonly assumed) one for the soul and the other for the body. Exclusive devotion to either produces imbalance -- savagery or weakness.
distilled: Wisdom belongs to the virtuous; both music and gymnastic aim at the soul; exclusive devotion to either produces dangerous imbalance.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Vice cannot know virtue' but virtue can know vice: asymmetric moral epistemology -- The harsh policy toward the incurable (bodily: leave to die; morally: put to death) is one of the Republic's most disturbing passages -- The revelation that both music and gymnastic serve the soul is a major philosophical insight, overturning conventional Greek educational theory -- The savage/soft dichotomy maps onto the spirited/philosophical duality in the soul -- Cross-reference: Book IV (439d-441c) on the three parts of the soul; Book IX (571c-572b) on the spirited element; Timaeus (87c-88b) on soul-body harmony

narrative:
"Yet surely," I said, "this ferocity only comes from spirit, which, if rightly educated, would give courage, but, if too much intensified, is liable to become hard and brutal."

"That I quite think."

"On the other hand the philosopher will have the quality of gentleness. And this also, when too much indulged, will turn to softness, but, if educated rightly, will be gentle and moderate."

"True."

"And in our opinion the guardians ought to have both these qualities?"

"Assuredly."

"And both should be in harmony?"

"Beyond question."

"And the harmonious soul is both temperate and courageous?"
refashioned:
"Yet this ferocity," I said, "comes from spirit -- which, properly educated, produces courage, but when overstimulated becomes hard and brutal."

"I agree."

"The philosophical nature, on the other hand, tends toward gentleness. Properly educated, this becomes genuine moderation; overindulged, it turns to weakness."

"True."

"And our guardians need both qualities?"

"Absolutely."

"And both must be in harmony?"

"Without question."

"And the harmonious soul is both temperate and courageous?"
elegant: Spirit rightly educated becomes courage; overintensified, it becomes brutality. Gentleness rightly educated becomes moderation; overindulged, it becomes weakness. Guardians need both qualities in harmony, producing a soul that is both temperate and courageous.
summary: Socrates shows that the spirited element, properly educated, becomes courage (but overstimulated becomes brutality), while the philosophical/gentle element, properly educated, becomes moderation (but overindulged becomes weakness). Guardians need both in harmony, producing a soul that is both temperate and courageous.
distilled: Spirit rightly educated becomes courage; gentleness rightly educated becomes moderation; guardians need both in harmony.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The spirited (thumoeides) and philosophical (philosophon) elements correspond to two parts of the soul developed more fully in Book IV -- 'Hard and brutal' vs. 'soft' = the extremes that balanced education prevents -- The harmonious soul combining temperance and courage is the educational ideal -- The two virtues (courage and temperance) are the ones earlier associated with the Dorian and Phrygian harmonies (3.36) -- Cross-reference: Book IV (441e-442d) on the harmonious soul; Book II (375a-376c) on the guardian's need for both spiritedness and gentleness

narrative:
"Yes."

"And the inharmonious is cowardly and boorish?"

"Very true."

"And, when a man allows music to play upon him and to pour into his soul through the funnel of his ears those sweet and soft and melancholy airs of which we were just now speaking, and his whole life is passed in warbling and the delights of song; in the first stage of the process the passion or spirit which is in him is tempered like iron, and made useful, instead of brittle and useless. But, if he carries on the softening and soothing process, in the next stage he begins to melt and waste, until he has wasted away his spirit and cut out the sinews of his soul; and he becomes a feeble warrior."
refashioned:
"Yes."

"And the disharmonious soul is cowardly and crude?"

"Very true."

"Now, when a person gives himself over to music -- letting those sweet, soft, melancholy airs pour into his soul through the funnel of his ears, spending his whole life immersed in singing and the pleasures of song -- at first the process is beneficial. The passion or spirit within him is tempered like iron, made useful rather than brittle.

But if the softening continues unchecked, in the next stage he begins to melt and waste away. His spirit dissolves. The sinews of his soul are cut. And he becomes a feeble warrior."
elegant: Music first tempers the spirit like iron, making it useful. But unrestrained musical indulgence continues the softening until spirit dissolves entirely, the sinews of the soul are severed, and the person becomes a feeble warrior incapable of action.
summary: Socrates describes how music initially tempers the spirit beneficially, like the tempering of iron. But if musical softening continues unchecked, it melts and wastes the spirit, cutting the sinews of the soul and producing a feeble warrior incapable of courage.
distilled: Music first tempers the soul's spirit like iron; continued unchecked, it melts spirit away entirely, producing a feeble warrior.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The iron-tempering metaphor is precise: the right amount of heat makes iron useful; too much makes it weak -- 'Through the funnel of his ears' is a vivid physical image of musical absorption -- 'Cut out the sinews of his soul' (neura ektemnei tes psyches) is one of Plato's most powerful metaphors -- The process has two stages: beneficial tempering, then destructive over-softening -- Cross-reference: Book X (606a-b) on how poetry 'waters' the emotions that should be dried; Phaedrus (245a) on divine madness and inspiration

narrative:
"Very true."

"If the element of spirit is naturally weak in him the change is speedily accomplished, but if he have a good deal, then the power of music weakening the spirit renders him excitable; -- on the least provocation he flames up at once, and is speedily extinguished; instead of having spirit he grows irritable and passionate and is quite impracticable."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"If his spirit is naturally weak, the transformation happens quickly. But if he has a strong spirit to begin with, the weakening effect of excessive music makes him volatile. At the slightest provocation he flares up, then immediately burns out. Instead of genuine spirit, he develops irritability and irrational passion -- he becomes completely impossible to deal with."
elegant: Those with weak spirits are quickly ruined by musical excess. Those with strong spirits are made volatile -- flaring up at the slightest provocation, then immediately burning out, replacing genuine courage with empty irritability.
summary: Socrates distinguishes two effects of musical excess depending on natural temperament: those with weak spirits are quickly made feeble, while those with naturally strong spirits become volatile and irritable -- flaring up and burning out quickly, replacing genuine courage with empty emotional reactivity.
distilled: Musical excess makes the weak-spirited feeble and the strong-spirited volatile, replacing genuine courage with empty irritability in both cases.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The distinction between weak and strong spirits facing the same corrupting influence shows Plato's psychological sophistication -- 'Flames up and is speedily extinguished' = the signature of a corrupted spirit: intense but unsustainable emotional reactions -- 'Irritable and passionate' (akracholos kai thumoeides) replaces genuine courage (andreia) -- The difference between genuine spirit and mere irritability is crucial -- Cross-reference: Book IV (440c-441a) on the spirited element of the soul; Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics IV.5 on appropriate vs. inappropriate anger

narrative:
"Exactly."

"And so in gymnastics, if a man takes violent exercise and is a great feeder, and the reverse of a great student of music and philosophy, at first the high condition of his body fills him with pride and spirit, and he becomes twice the man that he was."

"Certainly."

"And what happens? if he do nothing else, and holds no converse with the Muses, does not even that intelligence which there may be in him, having no taste of any sort of learning or enquiry or thought or culture, grow feeble and dull and blind, his mind never waking up or receiving nourishment, and his senses not being purged of their mists?"

"True," he said.

"And he ends by becoming a hater of philosophy, uncivilized, never using the weapon of persuasion, -- he is like a wild beast, all violence and fierceness, and knows no other way of dealing; and he lives in all ignorance and evil conditions, and has no sense of propriety and grace."

"That is quite true," he said.

"And as there are two principles of human nature, one the spirited and the other the philosophical, some God, as I should say, has given mankind two arts answering to them (and only indirectly to the soul and body), in order that these two principles (like the strings of an instrument) may be relaxed or drawn tighter until they are duly harmonised."

"That appears to be the intention."

"And he who mingles music with gymnastic in the fairest proportions, and best attempers them to the soul, may be rightly called the true musician and harmonist in a far higher sense than the tuner of the strings."

"You are quite right, Socrates."

"And such a presiding genius will be always required in our State if the government is to last."

"Yes, he will be absolutely necessary."

"Such, then, are our principles of nurture and education: Where would be the use of going into further details about the dances of our citizens, or about their hunting and coursing, their gymnastic and equestrian contests? For these all follow the general principle, and having found that, we shall have no difficulty in discovering them."
refashioned:
"Exactly."

"Now consider the opposite: gymnastics without music. If a man exercises violently, eats heavily, and never studies music or philosophy, at first his superb physical condition fills him with pride and energy. He feels twice the man he was."

"Certainly."

"But if he does nothing else -- never engages with the Muses, never tastes learning, inquiry, thought, or culture -- whatever intelligence he has grows feeble, dull, and blind. His mind never wakes up, never receives nourishment, his perceptions are never cleared of their fog."

"True," he said.

"He ends up hating philosophy. He becomes uncivilized, never using the weapon of persuasion. He's like a wild beast -- all violence and ferocity, knowing no other way. He lives in ignorance and degradation, with no sense of propriety or grace."

"Quite true," he said.

"So there are two principles in human nature: the spirited and the philosophical. Some god, I would say, has given mankind two corresponding arts -- music and gymnastic -- aimed not directly at soul and body, but at these two principles. Like the strings of an instrument, the two principles must be loosened or tightened until they are properly harmonized."

"That seems to be the purpose."

"And the person who blends music and gymnastic in the best proportions, tuning them to the soul, may rightly be called the true musician and harmonist -- in a far higher sense than someone who merely tunes strings."

"You're quite right, Socrates."

"Such a person will always be needed in our state if the government is to endure."

"Absolutely necessary."

"These, then, are our principles of education. Why go into details about dances, hunting, riding, or athletic competitions? They all follow from the general principles we've established, and won't be hard to work out."
elegant: Gymnastics without music produces a savage who hates philosophy and knows only violence. Music and gymnastic correspond to the two principles of human nature -- the philosophical and the spirited -- and must be harmonized like strings on an instrument. The person who achieves this blend is the true musician in the highest sense. Such a person will always be needed if the state is to endure.
summary: Socrates shows that exclusive gymnastics produces an uncivilized brute who hates philosophy and uses only force. He reveals that music and gymnastic correspond to the two principles of human nature (philosophical and spirited) and must be harmonized. The person who achieves this balance is the true musician in the deepest sense, and such a person is essential for the state's survival.
distilled: Music and gymnastic serve the two principles of human nature -- philosophical and spirited; their proper harmony produces the true musician, essential for the state's survival.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The symmetry is complete: excess music = soft/weak; excess gymnastics = hard/brutal; proper blend = harmonious soul -- 'Like the strings of an instrument' uses musical language to describe the educational goal -- 'The true musician and harmonist' = not a player of instruments but a tuner of souls -- 'A presiding genius' needed for the state = the philosopher-ruler anticipated -- The details (dances, hunting, equestrian) are deferred as derivative -- Cross-reference: Book IV (441e-442d) on psychic harmony; Book VII (521c-540b) on the philosopher-ruler's education; Statesman (305e-306a) on the weaving of spirited and gentle natures

narrative:
"I dare say that there will be no difficulty."

"Very good," I said; "then what is the next question? Must we not ask who are to be rulers and who subjects?"

"Certainly."

"There can be no doubt that the elder must rule the younger."

"Clearly."

"And that the best of these must rule."

"That is also clear."

"Now, are not the best husbandmen those who are most devoted to husbandry?"

"Yes."

"And as we are to have the best of guardians for our city, must they not be those who have most the character of guardians?"

"Yes."

"And to this end they ought to be wise and efficient, and to have a special care of the State?"

"True."

"And a man will be most likely to care about that which he loves?"

"To be sure."

"And he will be most likely to love that which he regards as having the same interests with himself, and that of which the good or evil fortune is supposed by him at any time most to affect his own?"

"Very true," he replied.

"Then there must be a selection. Let us note among the guardians those who in their whole life show the greatest eagerness to do what is for the good of their country, and the greatest repugnance to do what is against her interests."

"Those are the right men."

"And they will have to be watched at every age, in order that we may see whether they preserve their resolution, and never, under the influence either of force or enchantment, forget or cast off their sense of duty to the State."

"How cast off?" he said.

"I will explain to you," I replied. "A resolution may go out of a man's mind either with his will or against his will; with his will when he gets rid of a falsehood and learns better, against his will whenever he is deprived of a truth."

"I understand," he said, "the willing loss of a resolution; the meaning of the unwilling I have yet to learn."

"Why," I said, "do you not see that men are unwillingly deprived of good, and willingly of evil? Is not to have lost the truth an evil, and to possess the truth a good? and you would agree that to conceive things as they are is to possess the truth?"
refashioned:
"I expect not."

"Good. What comes next? We need to determine who will be rulers and who will be ruled."

"Certainly."

"Elders must rule the younger."

"Obviously."

"And the best among them must rule."

"Also clear."

"Just as the best farmers are the most devoted to farming, the best guardians must be those most devoted to guarding."

"Yes."

"They should be wise, capable, and genuinely care about the state."

"True."

"And a person cares most about what he loves."

"Of course."

"And he loves most what he believes shares his own interests -- whose fortune and misfortune he feels as his own."

"Very true," he replied.

"So we must select guardians who, throughout their entire lives, show the greatest eagerness to serve the state's good and the greatest revulsion against harming it."

"Those are the right people."

"And they must be observed at every age to see whether they maintain their commitment and never, through force or enchantment, abandon their sense of duty."

"What do you mean by 'cast off'?" he said.

"A conviction can leave a person's mind either willingly or unwillingly. Willingly, when he abandons a falsehood upon learning the truth. Unwillingly, when he is robbed of a truth."

"I understand the willing loss," he said, "but the unwilling loss needs explanation."

"Don't you see that people are unwillingly deprived of good things and willingly give up bad ones? Losing the truth is an evil. Possessing the truth is a good. And you'd agree that conceiving things as they are means possessing the truth?"
elegant: The best rulers are those who care most about the state, identify their interests with its interests, and maintain their convictions throughout life. We must test them at every age against force and enchantment. A conviction can be lost willingly (abandoning falsehood for truth) or unwillingly (being robbed of truth) -- and no one is willingly deprived of what is good.
summary: Socrates turns to selecting rulers from among the guardians. The best rulers are those who identify their interests completely with the state's, showing lifelong devotion. They must be tested at every age to ensure they maintain their convictions against force and enchantment. Socrates distinguishes between voluntary loss of conviction (correcting a falsehood) and involuntary loss (being robbed of truth).
distilled: The best rulers identify their interests with the state's and must be tested throughout life to ensure they never lose their true convictions through force, deception, or enchantment.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The transition from education to selection of rulers marks a new phase of the argument -- The principle that people love what shares their interests grounds political authority in alignment of interests -- Three threats to conviction: force (bia), enchantment (goeteia), and theft (klope) -- developed in 3.72 -- 'No one is willingly deprived of good' connects to the Socratic paradox that no one errs willingly -- Cross-reference: Book V (473c-d) on philosopher-rulers; Book VII (519c-521b) on rulers who don't want to rule; Protagoras (345d-e) on the involuntary nature of error

narrative:
"Yes," he replied; "I agree with you in thinking that mankind are deprived of truth against their will."

"And is not this involuntary deprivation caused either by theft, or force, or enchantment?"

"Still," he replied, "I do not understand you."

"I fear that I must have been talking darkly, like the tragedians. I only mean that some men are changed by persuasion and that others forget; argument steals away the hearts of one class, and time of the other; and this I call theft. Now you understand me?"

"Yes."

"Those again who are forced are those whom the violence of some pain or grief compels to change their opinion."

"I understand," he said, "and you are quite right."

"And you would also acknowledge that the enchanted are those who change their minds either under the softer influence of pleasure, or the sterner influence of fear?"

"Yes," he said; "everything that deceives may be said to enchant."
refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "I agree that people are deprived of truth against their will."

"And this involuntary deprivation happens through theft, force, or enchantment."

"I still don't follow," he said.

"I'm afraid I'm being obscure, like the tragedians. Let me be plain. Some people lose their convictions through persuasion, others through forgetting. Bad arguments steal the hearts of the first group; time steals from the second. That's what I mean by 'theft.' Do you understand now?"

"Yes."

"Those who lose convictions through 'force' are people whom intense pain or grief compels to change their minds."

"I understand, and you're right."

"And the 'enchanted' are those who change their minds under the seductive influence of pleasure or the intimidating influence of fear."

"Yes," he said. "Everything that deceives can be called enchantment."
elegant: Truth is lost in three ways: by theft (persuasion or forgetting steal it), by force (pain or grief compel change), or by enchantment (pleasure seduces or fear intimidates). Everything that deceives may be called enchantment.
summary: Socrates explains the three ways people involuntarily lose true convictions: theft (persuasion or time steals them away), force (pain or grief compels a change of mind), and enchantment (pleasure seduces or fear intimidates into abandoning truth). Glaucon adds that everything which deceives may be called enchantment.
distilled: True convictions are lost through three forms of involuntary deprivation: theft by persuasion, force through pain, and enchantment through pleasure or fear.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The three-fold classification (theft/force/enchantment) provides the framework for testing future rulers -- 'Talking darkly, like the tragedians' is self-deprecating humor about his own obscurity -- Theft = argument or time eroding conviction -- Force = pain/grief overwhelming conviction -- Enchantment = pleasure/fear seducing conviction -- 'Everything that deceives may be said to enchant' (Glaucon's contribution) is an important philosophical insight -- Cross-reference: Sophist (228c-d) on the types of ignorance; Phaedrus (261a-262c) on rhetoric as enchantment

narrative:
"Therefore, as I was just now saying, we must enquire who are the best guardians of their own conviction that what they think the interest of the State is to be the rule of their lives. We must watch them from their youth upwards, and make them perform actions in which they are most likely to forget or to be deceived, and he who remembers and is not deceived is to be selected, and he who falls in the trial is to be rejected. That will be the way?"

"Yes."

"And there should also be toils and pains and conflicts prescribed for them, in which they will be made to give further proof of the same qualities."
refashioned:
"So, as I was saying, we must find who are the best guardians of their own conviction that the state's interest must govern their lives. We must observe them from youth, putting them through situations where they're most likely to forget their principles or be deceived. Those who remember and resist deception are selected; those who fail are rejected."

"Yes."

"We should also prescribe hardships, suffering, and competitive struggles to test these same qualities further."
elegant: Future rulers must be tested from youth in situations designed to make them forget or abandon their principles. Those who remain steadfast through trials of deception, hardship, and competition are selected; those who fail are rejected.
summary: Socrates outlines the testing process for selecting rulers: they must be observed from youth through situations designed to make them forget or betray their principles, including hardships, pains, and competitive struggles. Only those who maintain their convictions are selected.
distilled: Future rulers must be tested from youth through trials of deception and hardship; only those who maintain their convictions are selected.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The testing program is comprehensive: cognitive (deception), physical (toils and pains), and competitive (conflicts) -- Selection by elimination through testing anticipates modern assessment processes -- The emphasis on lifelong observation ('from their youth upwards') makes this a continuous evaluation, not a one-time test -- Cross-reference: Book VII (537a-540a) on the extended education and testing of philosopher-rulers; Laws 751c-752a on testing officials

narrative:
"Very right," he replied.

"And then," I said, "we must try them with enchantments -- that is the third sort of test -- and see what will be their behaviour: like those who take colts amid noise and tumult to see if they are of a timid nature, so must we take our youth amid terrors of some kind, and again pass them into pleasures, and prove them more thoroughly than gold is proved in the furnace, that we may discover whether they are armed against all enchantments, and of a noble bearing always, good guardians of themselves and of the music which they have learned, and retaining under all circumstances a rhythmical and harmonious nature, such as will be most serviceable to the individual and to the State. And he who at every age, as boy and youth and in mature life, has come out of the trial victorious and pure, shall be appointed a ruler and guardian of the State; he shall be honoured in life and death, and shall receive sepulture and other memorials of honour, the greatest that we have to give. But him who fails, we must reject. I am inclined to think that this is the sort of way in which our rulers and guardians should be chosen and appointed. I speak generally, and not with any pretension to exactness."
refashioned:
"Very right," he replied.

"Then we must test them with enchantments -- the third type. Like breeders who take young horses into noise and chaos to see if they're skittish, we must expose our youth to terrors and then to pleasures, testing them more rigorously than gold in a furnace.

We need to discover whether they're proof against all enchantments, maintaining noble composure in every situation -- true guardians of themselves and of the musical education they've received, keeping a rhythmical and harmonious nature under all circumstances, the kind most useful to themselves and to the state.

Whoever emerges victorious and pure from these trials at every age -- as boy, as young man, and in full maturity -- shall be appointed ruler and guardian of the state. He shall be honored in life and in death, receiving the greatest memorials of honor we can bestow. But whoever fails, we reject.

This is roughly how I think our rulers should be chosen. I'm speaking in general terms, without claiming precision."
elegant: Like testing colts in chaos, we must expose candidates to terrors and pleasures, proving them more thoroughly than gold in a furnace. Those who emerge from every trial -- in boyhood, youth, and maturity -- maintaining their harmonious nature and noble bearing, are appointed rulers. Those who fail are rejected.
summary: Socrates completes the selection process for rulers with the test of enchantment: candidates are exposed to terrors and pleasures at every stage of life, tested more rigorously than gold in a furnace. Only those who maintain their principles, composure, and harmonious nature through every trial are appointed as rulers and honored in life and death.
distilled: Rulers are selected by lifelong testing through terrors and pleasures, more rigorously than gold in a furnace; only those who maintain their principles at every age are appointed.
notes: Speaker: Socrates -- The colt metaphor is vivid: young horses are tested for temperament in chaotic conditions -- 'More thoroughly than gold is proved in the furnace' = a standard of purity testing -- The testing at three ages (boy, youth, maturity) establishes a lifelong evaluation process -- 'Good guardians of themselves and of the music they have learned' links self-mastery to cultural education -- The modesty of 'I speak generally' is characteristic Socratic qualification -- Cross-reference: Book VII (537a-540a) provides a more detailed timeline for philosophical education and testing; Book VI (503b-504a) on the qualities needed for rulers

narrative:
"And, speaking generally, I agree with you," he said.

"And perhaps the word 'guardian' in the fullest sense ought to be applied to this higher class only who preserve us against foreign enemies and maintain peace among our citizens at home, that the one may not have the will, or the others the power, to harm us. The young men whom we before called guardians may be more properly designated auxiliaries and supporters of the principles of the rulers."
refashioned:
"Speaking generally, I agree with you," he said.

"And perhaps the title 'guardian' in its fullest sense should be reserved for this higher class alone -- those who protect us from external enemies and maintain domestic peace, ensuring that outsiders lack the power and insiders lack the will to harm us. The young warriors we previously called guardians would be more properly termed 'auxiliaries' -- supporters and enforcers of the rulers' principles."
elegant: The title 'guardian' properly belongs only to the tested rulers who protect against external enemies and maintain internal peace. The younger warriors are more accurately called 'auxiliaries' -- enforcers of the rulers' principles.
summary: Socrates refines the terminology: 'guardian' in its fullest sense applies only to the proven rulers who protect against external threats and maintain internal order. The younger warriors previously called guardians are properly 'auxiliaries' -- supporters and enforcers of the rulers' principles.
distilled: True 'guardians' are the tested rulers; the younger warriors are 'auxiliaries' who support and enforce the rulers' principles.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage establishes the crucial distinction between guardians proper (phylakes) and auxiliaries (epikouroi) -- The guardian class is now split into two: rulers (who have passed all tests) and warriors (who support them) -- External enemies + internal order = the two functions of the ruling class -- 'The one may not have the will' (internal) and 'the others the power' (external) to harm = different threats require different responses -- Cross-reference: Book IV (428a-429a) where the guardian/auxiliary distinction is important for defining wisdom and courage in the state

narrative:
"I agree with you," he said.

"How then may we devise one of those needful falsehoods of which we lately spoke -- just one royal lie which may deceive the rulers, if that be possible, and at any rate the rest of the city?"

"What sort of lie?" he said.

"Nothing new," I replied; "only an old Phoenician tale of what has often occurred before now in other places, (as the poets say, and have made the world believe,) though not in our time, and I do not know whether such an event could ever happen again, or could now even be made probable, if it did."

"How your words seem to hesitate on your lips!"

"You will not wonder," I replied, "at my hesitation when you have heard."
refashioned:
"I agree," he said.

"Now, how might we craft one of those necessary falsehoods we discussed earlier -- a single 'royal lie' that could convince the rulers themselves, if possible, and certainly the rest of the city?"

"What kind of lie?" he said.

"Nothing original," I replied. "Just an old Phoenician story about something that has supposedly happened in other places -- or so the poets say, and they've made people believe it -- though not in our time. I don't know whether such a thing could ever happen again or be made believable if it did."

"You seem to be hesitating!"

"You won't be surprised at my hesitation once you hear what it is."
elegant: Socrates proposes devising a 'royal lie' -- a necessary falsehood to unite the city. He presents it as an old Phoenician tale, already hesitating at the audacity of what he's about to suggest.
summary: Socrates introduces the famous 'noble lie' or 'royal lie,' presenting it hesitantly as an old Phoenician tale that must be devised to unite the city. His visible discomfort signals the audacity of what follows -- a founding myth designed to convince even the rulers, if possible, and certainly the rest of the citizenry.
distilled: Socrates hesitantly introduces the 'noble lie' -- a necessary founding myth to unite the city, presented as an old Phoenician tale.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'noble lie' (gennaion pseudos) or 'royal lie' (literally 'well-born falsehood') is one of the most debated concepts in political philosophy -- 'Phoenician tale' may allude to Cadmus founding Thebes with earth-born soldiers -- Socrates' dramatic hesitation ('How your words seem to hesitate') makes the passage theatrical -- The lie is described as 'needful' (anagkaion), connecting to the earlier permission for rulers to lie for the public good (3.9) -- Cross-reference: Book II (382c-d) on the 'lie in the soul' vs. 'lie in words'; Laws 663d-e on beneficial falsehood; Karl Popper and Leo Strauss debate the political implications

narrative:
"Speak," he said, "and fear not."

"Well then, I will speak, although I really know not how to look you in the face, or in what words to utter the audacious fiction, which I propose to communicate gradually, first to the rulers, then to the soldiers, and lastly to the people. They are to be told that their youth was a dream, and the education and training which they received from us, an appearance only; in reality during all that time they were being formed and fed in the womb of the earth, where they themselves and their arms and appurtenances were manufactured; when they were completed, the earth, their mother, sent them up; and so, their country being their mother and also their nurse, they are bound to advise for her good, and to defend her against attacks, and her citizens they are to regard as children of the earth and their own brothers."

"You had good reason," he said, "to be ashamed of the lie which you were going to tell."
refashioned:
"Speak," he said, "and don't be afraid."

"Well, I'll speak, though I hardly know how to look you in the eye or find words for this audacious fiction. I propose to communicate it gradually -- first to the rulers, then to the soldiers, and finally to the people.

They are to be told that their youth was a dream and their education merely an illusion. In reality, during all that time they were being formed and nourished in the womb of the earth, where they and their weapons and equipment were fashioned. When they were complete, the earth -- their mother -- sent them up to the surface.

Therefore, since the earth is their mother and nurse, they are bound to protect her and defend her against all attacks. And they must regard their fellow citizens as brothers -- children of the same earth."

"You had good reason to be embarrassed about this lie," he said.
elegant: The noble lie: everyone is told their education was a dream; in reality they were formed in the earth's womb and sent up complete. Therefore the earth is their mother, the country their nurse, and all citizens are brothers born from the same soil. This myth binds citizens to their land and to each other.
summary: Socrates reveals the first part of the noble lie: citizens are to believe their education was a dream and that they were actually formed in the earth's womb, making the country their mother. This creates a mythic bond between citizens and their land, and establishes all citizens as siblings -- children of the earth.
distilled: The noble lie's first part: all citizens were born from the earth, making their country their mother and all citizens brothers, bonding them to land and to each other.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The earth-born myth (autochthony) was a real element of Athenian civic ideology: Athenians believed they were 'autochthonous,' born from the soil of Attica -- The myth serves three functions: (1) bonds citizens to the land, (2) creates fraternity among citizens, (3) erases the artificiality of the social order -- Glaucon's response ('good reason to be ashamed') shows the lie's audacity even to a sympathetic listener -- 'Gradually, first to the rulers' = the lie must percolate down the social hierarchy -- Cross-reference: Menexenus (237b-238a) where Plato parodies Athenian autochthony myths; Timaeus (23d-24a) on Egyptian criticism of Greek origin stories

narrative:
"True," I replied, "but there is more coming; I have only told you half. Citizens, we shall say to them in our tale, you are brothers, yet God has framed you differently. Some of you have the power of command, and in the composition of these he has mingled gold, wherefore also they have the greatest honour; others he has made of silver, to be auxiliaries; others again who are to be husbandmen and craftsmen he has composed of brass and iron; and the species will generally be preserved in the children. But as all are of the same original stock, a golden parent will sometimes have a silver son, or a silver parent a golden son. And God proclaims as a first principle to the rulers, and above all else, that there is nothing which they should so anxiously guard, or of which they are to be such good guardians, as of the purity of the race. They should observe what elements mingle in their offspring; for if the son of a golden or silver parent has an admixture of brass and iron, then nature orders a transposition of ranks, and the eye of the ruler must not be pitiful towards the child because he has to descend in the scale and become a husbandman or artisan, just as there may be sons of artisans who having an admixture of gold or silver in them are raised to honour, and become guardians or auxiliaries. For an oracle says that when a man of brass or iron guards the State, it will be destroyed. Such is the tale; is there any possibility of making our citizens believe in it?"

"Not in the present generation," he replied; "there is no way of accomplishing this; but their sons may be made to believe in the tale, and their sons' sons, and posterity after them."
refashioned:
"True," I replied, "but there's more. I've only told you half.

In our tale, we'll say to the citizens: 'You are all brothers, but God has made you differently. Some of you have the capacity for leadership -- in their composition God mixed gold, which is why they have the greatest honor. Others are made of silver and serve as auxiliaries. Still others -- the farmers and craftsmen -- are composed of brass and iron.

The metal will generally pass from parent to child. But since everyone comes from the same stock, a golden parent may sometimes have a silver child, or a silver parent a golden one.

God's first commandment to the rulers, above all else, is this: nothing is more important for them to guard than the mixture of metals in the next generation. If a golden or silver parent produces a child with brass or iron, the child must be demoted without pity to the rank of farmer or craftsman. And if a craftsman's child shows gold or silver, that child must be elevated to guardian or auxiliary.

For an oracle declares that the state will be destroyed when it is guarded by a man of brass or iron.'

That's the tale. Is there any chance our citizens would believe it?"

"Not the present generation," he replied. "There's no way to accomplish that. But their children might believe it, and their children's children, and all posterity after them."
elegant: The second part of the noble lie: God has mixed gold into rulers, silver into auxiliaries, brass and iron into workers. The metals generally pass to children, but mobility between classes must be maintained based on each child's nature. An oracle warns that the state falls when guarded by men of brass or iron. The present generation won't believe it, but future generations might.
summary: Socrates reveals the second part of the noble lie: a myth of metals in which God has composed rulers of gold, auxiliaries of silver, and workers of brass and iron. While the metals usually pass to children, social mobility must be maintained based on each individual's nature. An oracle warns that the state will fall if the wrong metal guards it. Glaucon says the present generation won't believe it, but future ones might.
distilled: The myth of metals: rulers are gold, auxiliaries silver, workers brass and iron, with mobility between classes based on individual nature; the state falls when guarded by the wrong metal.
